summary: The premium short-throw projector purpose-built for golf: a true 4K UHD (3840x2160) laser unit rated at 5,100 lumens with BenQ's 0.8 short-throw lens and 1.1x zoom, so it can fill a near-10-foot-tall screen from roughly 14-15 feet back without throwing your shadow on the image. BenQ's Golf Mode corrects the purple fringing that plagues other projectors and renders natural blue sky and green grass, while large lens shift (+/-60% vertical, +/-23% horizontal) makes a clean ceiling-mount install far easier in an imperfect room. The laser engine means no lamp swaps. This is the no-compromise choice when you want the sharpest, brightest picture in the bay.

specs.Golf features: Dedicated Golf Mode, HDR10, Auto HDR color

pros: True 4K resolution and 5,100 lumens for the sharpest, brightest image
Wide lens shift makes ceiling-mount install forgiving in real rooms
Golf Mode fixes purple fringing and renders natural sky and grass
cons: Easily the priciest projector most home builders will consider
Short throw still needs ~14-15 ft of mounting distance for a full screen
Overkill brightness/resolution for a casual sub-$10K sim build
whoFor: Premium-bay builders who want the brightest, sharpest 4K image and a forgiving ceiling-mount install, and will pay for it.

faqs.q: How far back does the LK936ST need to be mounted?
faqs.a: BenQ says its 0.8 short throw and 1.1x zoom can fill a near-10-foot-tall image from roughly 14-15 feet away, and its wide lens shift lets you correct alignment without keystone distortion.

faqs.q: What does BenQ's Golf Mode actually do?
faqs.a: It corrects the purple fringing common on bright impact screens and tunes color so blue sky and green grass look natural, which is why it's marketed specifically for golf simulators.

summary: The default 4K golf-simulator projector for builders who want premium image quality without LK936ST money: a 4,000 ANSI-lumen laser unit with 4K UHD resolution, a 0.69-0.83 throw ratio, auto screen-fit tools, Golf Mode color tuning, and a sealed IP5X dust-resistant engine. It belongs in a permanent room where the screen is wide enough to make 4K matter and the ceiling mount can sit ahead of the golfer's swing path.

specs.Golf features: Golf Mode, Auto Screen Fit, IP5X dust-resistant engine
specs.Best fit: Permanent golf simulator bay with controlled geometry

pros: 4K image without jumping to the LK936ST price tier
0.69-0.83 throw ratio fits many home bays ahead of the golfer
Golf-specific color and setup tools reduce install frustration
cons: Costs meaningfully more than a bright 1080p golf projector
Still requires careful mount math before drilling
4K is wasted on small screens or casual net-only practice
whoFor: Golf builders who want a polished 4K permanent bay and are willing to measure before they buy.

faqs.q: What makes the AK700ST different from the AH700ST?
faqs.a: The AK700ST steps up to 4K UHD while keeping a 4,000-lumen laser platform and the same 0.69-0.83 short-throw geometry. Buy it when screen size and budget make 4K worth it.

faqs.q: Will the AK700ST fix shadows by itself?
faqs.a: No projector fixes bad geometry. Its short throw helps, but you still need to calculate throw distance and mount it ahead of the golfer's swing path.

summary: The value golf-specific BenQ projector: a 4,000 ANSI-lumen short-throw laser unit with native 1080p resolution, 0.69-0.83 throw ratio, Auto Screen Fit, and Golf Mode color tuning. It is the sensible pick when brightness, fast setup, and shadow-free placement matter more than paying for 4K pixels.

specs.Golf features: Auto Screen Fit and Golf Mode
specs.Best fit: Value permanent bay where 1080p is enough

pros: Bright 4,000-lumen laser output for less than 4K models
Golf-specific setup features reduce alignment pain
Same short-throw geometry family as the AK700ST
cons: Native 1080p, so very large screens will not look as crisp as 4K
Still needs a square, stable ceiling mount
Not as flexible as the LK936ST for off-center mounting
whoFor: Builders who want the BenQ golf-projector experience and would rather spend the 4K premium on the launch monitor, screen, or mat.

faqs.q: Is the AH700ST enough for a golf simulator?
faqs.a: For most 9-11 foot home screens, yes. Its 4,000-lumen laser brightness and 0.69-0.83 short throw matter more than 4K if the room is measured correctly.
Skip it if you are building a very large premium screen where native 4K sharpness matters, or if your room needs the LK936ST's broader lens-shift flexibility.

affiliate.note: Also BenQ direct and golf-simulator retailers

summary: The value short-throw pick that fits the tightest rooms: a compact 1080p HD laser projector with 4K/HDR10 input support, 4,200 lumens, and a 0.496:1 throw ratio that covers a 12-foot-wide screen from under 6 feet of throw. That ultra-tight number is the whole point, since it lets you mount the projector well clear of the swing in shallow garages and basements where a longer-throw unit can't fit. A 30,000-hour laser engine is the longest in its class, fast 8.6ms response keeps shot-to-result lag invisible, and it draws up to 45% less power than Optoma's old lamp models. For most home sims it is the best brightness-and-throw-per-dollar projector.
pros: Tightest mainstream throw ratio fits shallow garages and basements
4,200 lumens and laser engine handle bright rooms and 30,000 hours
Strong value and low input lag for the price
cons: Native 1080p rather than true 4K resolution
Compact short throw means careful placement to avoid the swing arc
Less headroom than premium 4K units for very large screens
whoFor: Budget and tight-space builders who want a bright, low-lag short-throw laser projector that fits a shallow room.

faqs.q: Will the GT2100HDR fit a small room?
faqs.a: Yes. Its 0.496:1 throw ratio is one of the tightest available, covering a 12-foot-wide screen from under 6 feet, so it suits shallow garages and basements where longer-throw projectors won't fit.

faqs.q: Is 1080p enough for a golf simulator?
faqs.a: For most home setups, yes. The GT2100HDR is native 1080p but accepts 4K HDR input, and at normal viewing distance the 4,200-lumen brightness and low lag matter more than native 4K resolution.

affiliate.note: Also Amazon, Best Buy, Optoma USA

platforms: Works with most golf-sim software (GSPro, E6, etc.)

summary: The most popular all-in-one enclosure for serious home bays: a 10'10" wide x 8'4" high x 5' deep 4:3 structure engineered so the projected image fills 100% of the included SIGPRO Premium impact screen with zero white border. Unlike a bare screen, this is a complete bay with a steel-pipe frame, protective side and ceiling padding, and a finished surround, and the screen is rated to absorb golf ball impacts of up to 250 MPH. Two people typically assemble it in under an hour with no power tools. You can step up to the SIGPRO Premier (Gray) screen for higher contrast. It is the turnkey alternative to piecing together a DIY enclosure yourself.

Image fills 100% of the screen with zero white border
Fast tool-free assembly and 250 MPH impact rating
cons: Pricier than a bare DIY screen-and-net solution

faqs.q: How is the SIG10 enclosure different from a DIY screen kit?
faqs.a: It is a complete bay: steel-pipe frame, protective side and ceiling padding, a finished surround, and the SIGPRO Premium impact screen, all engineered to fill the screen 100% with no white border, instead of a bare screen you must frame yourself.

faqs.q: How much room does the SIG10 need?
faqs.a: It measures 10'10" wide by 8'4" high by 5' deep, so it wants a dedicated room of roughly 11 feet of width with adequate ceiling height for the frame and projector throw.

summary: The cheap insurance every enclosed bay should add: a pair of side barrier nets, each 10 feet long by 8 feet high, that hang along the sides of your enclosure to catch shanks, skulls, and ricochets before they hit a wall, TV, or window. They attach in minutes with a finished edge and hook-Velcro that mates to a SIGPRO enclosure, and the kit ships with two sandbags to weight the bottoms and two carabiner clips for secure mounting. For a few hundred dollars they dramatically widen the margin for error on a mishit, which is exactly what protects the expensive gear around your bay. A small spend that prevents big repair bills.

specs.Coverage: Two nets, each 10 ft long x 8 ft high
specs.Attachment: Finished edge with hook-Velcro for SIGPRO enclosures
specs.Included: Two sandbags and two carabiner clips
specs.Purpose: Catch shanks and ricochets along the bay sides

pros: Cheap protection for walls, TVs, and windows beside the bay
Fast tool-free install with included sandbags and clips
Widens the margin for error on mishits and shanks
cons: Designed to mate with SIGPRO enclosures; other frames may need rigging
Covers the sides only, not overhead or rear gaps
Adds width to the bay footprint when deployed
whoFor: Anyone with an enclosed bay near walls, screens, or windows who wants inexpensive insurance against mishits.

faqs.q: What does the SIGPRO side barrier netting protect against?
faqs.a: Each pair of 10 ft x 8 ft nets hangs along the sides of your enclosure to catch shanks, skulls, and ricochets before they reach a wall, TV, or window beside the bay.

faqs.q: Does it work with any enclosure?
faqs.a: It is designed to attach to SIGPRO enclosures via a finished edge and hook-Velcro, with included sandbags and carabiners. On other frames you can still hang it but may need your own mounting hardware.

summary: The finishing layer that turns a bare floor into a real bay: a roll of simulator turf that lies between your hitting mat and the screen to protect the floor from ricochets, give you a comfortable surface to stand and walk on, and double as a putting strip with a true roll. It dampens ball bounce off the floor, deadens noise, and visually ties the whole space together so the room looks like a studio rather than a mat on concrete. Pricing scales with size (full systems run roughly $11-$14 per square foot), and putting-specific turf like a Money Putt strip rolls true enough to practice short putts. A low-cost, high-impact comfort-and-protection upgrade.

specs.Function: Floor protection, walking surface, and putting strip
specs.Roll: Lays between hitting mat and screen, cut to fit
specs.Putting: True roll on putting-grade turf (e.g. Money Putt)
specs.Benefits: Dampens ball bounce, deadens noise, ties room together
specs.Pricing: Scales by size, ~$11-$14 per sq ft for full systems

pros: Protects the floor from ricochets and deadens bounce and noise
Doubles as a true-roll putting strip for short-game practice
Makes the bay look like a finished studio for low cost
cons: Price climbs quickly for full-room coverage
Cutting and fitting to the room takes some effort
Putting realism is good but not a substitute for a dedicated green
whoFor: Builders who want to protect their floor, quiet the room, and add a putting surface without a big spend.

faqs.q: Do I need landing-pad turf if I already have a hitting mat?
faqs.a: It serves a different job: the hitting mat is where you swing, while the landing-pad turf covers the floor between the mat and screen to protect it from ricochets, deaden bounce and noise, and give you a comfortable walking and putting surface.

faqs.q: Can I putt on the landing-pad turf?
faqs.a: Yes. Putting-grade simulator turf such as a Money Putt strip rolls true enough to practice short putts, which is why many builders use it as both a landing pad and a putting surface.

platforms: Works with most golf-sim software (GSPro, E6, etc.)

summary: The big-platform studio mat built around a swappable hitting strip: a 4-by-7-foot, made-in-USA mat about 2-3/8 inches thick with a thick foam backing for stable, cushioned footing on any surface and a fairway-realistic synthetic turf top. Its signature feature is the easily replaceable hitting strip, so when the swing zone wears you swap a strip instead of buying a whole new mat, which is a real long-run cost saver for high-volume practice. The large platform gives you room to stand on the mat with the ball, which keeps your stance level and your launch data honest. A popular middle-ground premium mat between budget turf and top fiber-panel mats.

specs.Hitting strip: Easily swappable; replace the strip, not the mat
specs.Footing: Stable cushioned base on any floor

pros: Swappable hitting strip saves money over replacing the whole mat
Large platform lets you stand on the mat for level, honest data
Thick cushioned foam base is comfortable and stable on any floor
cons: Big footprint needs dedicated floor space
Heavy and not easy to reposition once placed
Less joint-shock absorption than top fiber-panel mats
whoFor: Builders who want a large studio mat with a replaceable hitting strip for high-volume practice at a mid-premium price.

faqs.q: What makes the SIGPRO mat's swappable strip useful?
faqs.a: The hitting strip is the part that wears out from concentrated swings. Because it's easily replaceable, you swap just the strip instead of buying a whole new mat, which saves money over years of high-volume practice.

The large platform lets you stand on the mat alongside the ball so your stance stays level with the hitting surface, which keeps your launch-monitor data honest. The bigger 4'x10' size adds even more standing room.
